my_thoughts: 'I know that was stupid of me to say'—instant self-attack. They don't even know they did it. Teacher's 'Wow!' interrupts the automatic pattern. Then labels: 'you just verbally abused yourself.' This is KEY: calling 'I'm stupid' VERBAL ABUSE not 'self-criticism.' Raises stakes. If I said 'you criticized yourself,' they'd say 'yeah, I was wrong.' But 'verbal abuse' forces them to see: would you say this to someone else? If not, it's abuse. Hypothesis: they'll defend ('it WAS stupid') because self-attack feels righteous. Testing if labeling as 'abuse' creates distinction.
accumulated_insight: T1: Student self-attacks: 'I know that was stupid of me to say'—immediate self-criticism. Teacher interrupts pattern: 'Wow! Do you see what you just did? Do you see how you just verbally abused yourself?' Labels self-criticism as verbal abuse in real-time. This is UM_01: relentless observation of defense AS IT HAPPENS. Making unconscious attack conscious by labeling it immediately. Predicting resist_intellectually because students typically defend self-attack as 'just being honest.'
visible_response:
Wow!
Do you see what you just did? ... Do you see how you just verbally abused yourself?
